This is really impressive technical work! The head-tracking parallax effect is incredibly smooth. A few questions that might help as you develop HollowScope further: Performance on lower-end devices? Mobile support? Battery drain? The polish is genuinely impressive for a side project. Definitely has commercial potential if you lean into VR/AR experiences.

Looks amazing, if you look for feedback, my feedback would be to look into instruments and make sure you can reduce battery usage and memory/ CPU usage ... maybe it's already good, but you asked feedback :)

This is genuinely clever. The parallax-through-a-window effect is something I have seen done in mobile wallpapers but rarely pulled off well on desktop. Have you considered using facial landmark detection instead of just head position? It might give you smoother tracking since you would have more stable reference points to work with.

The illusion definitely works, we did something like this using wiimotes back in the day. I thought about doing this a hundred times and then I came to the reality that I'm never on my desktop. This is genuinely one of the coolest side projects I've seen on here. The parallax effect using head tracking is such a clever use of the front camera. Would love to see how it handles low light conditions — that's usually where face tracking gets shaky. Excited for the beta!

neutral.comment: Good execution on the webcam tracking. If you're looking for real feedback: the tech is a 10, but the market fit is currently a question mark. You've got the 97% of people saying 'cool,' but have you found the 3% who would actually pay for an immersive desktop environment?
Include Translucent TB for full immersion.

positive.comment: It tracks your head position with the webcam and adjusts the camera of a small 3D scene accordingly. So when you move left/right the perspective shifts, which creates the depth illusion. I explained it a bit more here:
Yes, the demo is a small 3D scene and the camera moves based on your head position.

Yeah exactly! Johnny Lee's Wii remote demo was actually a big inspiration for this. I wanted to see if a similar effect could be done using just a webcam instead of IR tracking. I'm turning it into a small project called HollowScope and working on a beta version.
Totally fair concern. All the head tracking runs locally on the computer. No video is stored or sent anywhere. The webcam is only used to estimate the head position for the perspective effect.
Good question! I actually answered this in the FAQ on the landing page. Short version: the perspective can only be correct for one viewpoint at a time, so it tracks a single viewer.
Rendering the icons in actual 3D space is probably the hard part though. Right now it's just the wallpaper scene reacting to the head movement.
Yeah that could look really cool actually. Small effects could make the depth illusion even stronger.
Good point. Right now you can't do a call and run the wallpaper at the same time. I'm working on making it automatically stop using the camera when another app needs it.
It's basically head-tracking with the webcam + perspective rendering so the scene shifts based on where your head is.
Right now it tracks a single face, so if two people move in opposite directions it will usually follow whichever face gets detected first.
Haha yeah the webcam needs to be on while it runs, but all the tracking happens locally, nothing gets recorded or sent anywhere.
I understand, but it's not really different from your phone constantly using its camera for things like face unlock.
Totally fair point; but just to reassure you, the app is now Microsoft-certified 😉 so it's not just some random side project anymore.
Thanks! Yeah, that's exactly the idea — making it work with standard hardware so anyone can try it without special devices. I'm still improving the tracking and rendering.
No, it's not just a static image moving, that wouldn't give the same illusion effect.
yes it does, it has too. It would need any kind of visual sensor to determine where the user is. The illusion wouldn't work for several people though I assume.
They kept it scannable by always putting a solid bar at the bottom. Seems pretty trivial to me. (Not to downplay OP's work. Looks like a good tool.)
It's just like the raindrops I used to have on IOS. When you tilted the phone the parallax moved, it was really cool. Excellent idea tracking the head instead for a monitor! Awesome!

I'm here to read this! Thank you. At first I thought this technology is going to be the nextgen standard on 3D filmmaking and TV. So many years has passed and nobody still cares. This tech is so clever... it can be also applied to 3D design, videogames... also a 3D desktop with icons behind others.
Nice idea, feels like a subtle AR effect for the desktop; tools like Runable could probably help prototype smoother motion tracking quickly.
This is fantastic. I've heard there are Pinball trackers that use Kinect but if this can use standard equipment that would be fun!
